content.assessment: Removing only the age-10 entry point is a partial intervention in the selection funnel but leaves the core geographic insulation pattern (concentration of both 6- and 8-year tracks in urban/county-seat areas) almost entirely intact for the retained 8-year and 4-year tracks. The proposal's own risk acknowledgment—that displaced demand may concentrate more intensely at age-12—is consistent with a demographic reading in which the shrinking cohort pool competing for a reduced number of early-entry seats could increase competitive intensity per seat, even as absolute numbers fall due to the ~20% birth decline. The panel-data point that score evolution is only observable from grade 8 onward is relevant but contested as to mechanism (peer effects vs. teacher allocation vs. pedagogy), limiting confidence in claims that age-10 selection is demographically distinguishable from age-12 selection on ability-signal grounds.
opportunities: Could serve as an incremental testbed for observing whether demand displacement to age-12 entry produces measurable geographic or cohort-composition shifts, informing further phased reform
strengths: Targets the youngest and, per cross-national reasoning, potentially least ability-justified entry point for elimination
Explicitly anticipates a displacement-of-demand risk, which is a demographically coherent concern given a shrinking, geographically concentrated pool of eligible candidates
threats: A shrinking cohort concentrating around fewer remaining entry points could intensify competition and stakes at age-12 without net change in segregation, as the risk section itself notes
weaknesses: Retains the same urban/county-seat spatial concentration for the surviving 8-year and 4-year tracks, so the core geographic insulation from depopulation is not addressed
Evidence base is explicitly weak, and the ability-signal argument rests on contested mechanism attribution (peer effects, teacher allocation, or pedagogy)
